title: Karatsuba's Integer Multiplication
whenToUse: To beat $n^2$ for multiplication. Also a textbook example of saving a recursive call via algebraic identity → changing the exponent in the recurrence.
keyFormulas: So the middle term = $(x_1+x_0)(y_1+y_0) - x_1 y_1 - x_0 y_0$ — only 3 multiplications
professor:
**Setup.** Multiply two $n$-digit numbers. Schoolbook is $\Theta(n^2)$. The natural D&C split is $x = x_1 \cdot 10^{n/2} + x_0$, $y = y_1 \cdot 10^{n/2} + y_0$, giving
$$ xy = x_1 y_1 \cdot 10^n + (x_1 y_0 + x_0 y_1) \cdot 10^{n/2} + x_0 y_0. $$
A naive D&C uses four recursive multiplications ($x_1 y_1, x_1 y_0, x_0 y_1, x_0 y_0$) and $O(n)$ non-recursive work (adds and shifts), giving $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n = \Theta(n^2)$. No improvement over schoolbook.

**Karatsuba's observation.** You only need three quantities to reconstruct $xy$: $A = x_1 y_1$, $B = x_0 y_0$, and $C = (x_1 + x_0)(y_1 + y_0)$. Then the cross term is
$$ x_1 y_0 + x_0 y_1 \;=\; C - A - B. $$
So you make **three** recursive multiplications instead of four, plus some extra additions/subtractions (all $O(n)$). The recurrence drops to $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$, with solution $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.58})$.

**Algorithm.**

$$ \texttt{Karatsuba}(x, y): \text{ if } n = 1 \text{ return } x \cdot y.\ \text{Else split; } A \leftarrow \texttt{Karatsuba}(x_1, y_1);\ B \leftarrow \texttt{Karatsuba}(x_0, y_0);\ C \leftarrow \texttt{Karatsuba}(x_1{+}x_0, y_1{+}y_0);\ \text{return } A \cdot 10^n + (C - A - B) \cdot 10^{n/2} + B. $$

**Recurrence solution via recursion tree.** Level $i$: $3^i$ subproblems, each of size $n/2^i$, non-recursive work $n/2^i$, level work $3^i \cdot n/2^i = n (3/2)^i$. An **increasing** geometric series — dominated by the last level. Height $\log_2 n$; leaves count $3^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 3}$. Total $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$.

**Correctness.** Inductive: base case of single-digit multiplication is trivially correct; the formula
$$ xy = A \cdot 10^n + (C - A - B) \cdot 10^{n/2} + B $$
is an algebraic identity; the three recursive calls produce correct $A, B, C$ by I.H. Hence $xy$ is correctly computed.

**Why the trick matters.** The improvement comes from the exponent change: $4^{\log_2 n} = n^2$ vs $3^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 3}$. Saving one recursive call reduces the exponent from $2$ to $\log_2 3$.

**Historical note.** Kolmogorov conjectured $\Omega(n^2)$ was optimal in 1955. Karatsuba (a student) disproved it in 1960 with this algorithm. Follow-ups: Toom–Cook (more pieces, closer to $O(n)$), Schönhage–Strassen (FFT, $O(n \log n \log \log n)$), Harvey–van der Hoeven (2019, $O(n \log n)$).
simplified:
**Idea:** to multiply two $n$-digit numbers, split each in half. Schoolbook D&C needs 4 subproducts ($\Theta(n^2)$). Karatsuba gets by with 3.

**The trick:** instead of computing $x_1 y_0 + x_0 y_1$ as two products, compute $(x_1+x_0)(y_1+y_0)$ as one product. Then
$$ x_1 y_0 + x_0 y_1 = (x_1+x_0)(y_1+y_0) - x_1 y_1 - x_0 y_0, $$
which uses only quantities you'd already compute.

**Three recursive calls instead of four:**

- $A = x_1 y_1$
- $B = x_0 y_0$
- $C = (x_1 + x_0)(y_1 + y_0)$

Then $xy = A \cdot 10^n + (C - A - B) \cdot 10^{n/2} + B$.

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$. Level work is $n \cdot (3/2)^i$ — increasing geometric, dominated by the leaves. Total $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.58})$.

**Pattern:** whenever you can algebraically collapse $a$ subproblems into $a-1$ by computing a combined product, your exponent drops. Strassen's matrix multiplication does the same thing.
